comment.body:
I honestly don’t even know what to say at this point - but rather than focusing on public recognition on Twitter, I believe this might be a good moment to reflect on how communication with your subscribers could be improved.
Since early April, with the growing emphasis on geo-narratives, it feels like the sub has lost some of the clarity and practical value it previously offered. I genuinely feel for those who fully embraced the more extreme bearish views and ended up taking significant losses - several of them have shared their experiences openly in the chat.
It’s also unfortunate that people who struggle to interpret your posts are sometimes dismissed as inexperienced or naive. From my perspective, the intraday and weekly updates in recent weeks have become increasingly difficult to follow. The messaging often points in multiple directions and is later referenced with statements like “as I said….” Regardless of how we frame it, this sub either missed a sharp "bear market rally" - or a very effective "bull trap".
There’s been a consistent drumbeat around “risk/reward favors the short side,” with repeated downside targets like 4,400 or even 3,996. Phrases such as “we are not out of the woods”, "upcoming earnings (TSLA, GOOG this week) will be weak" have become routine. While I absolutely respect your analyses and your talent as a trader, I personally find that so-called “high conviction” trades - often accompanied by disclaimers like “very small allocation” or “big risk” - don’t offer the directional clarity I’m looking for, especially as someone who doesn’t trade short-term timeframes. And as you know, negative narratives tend to resonate more deeply - and linger longer - than positive ones.
Your recent comment - “The goal is to have money for your daily bread, not to be right on the market direction” - made it clear that directional accuracy may not be your primary objective. Looking back, the actual market behavior over the past month diverged significantly from what many of your analyses suggested (I think this will not be highlighted on twitter?). And now we’re seeing retrospective explanations that the market broke through key inflection points because of government interventions or a so-called “Trump Put.” As I understood - the market also fell in the past due to government actions. And if I interpreted it correctly, that “Trump Put” was supposedly on April 9?
Until the end of March, the learning curve here was steep but extremely valuable. The analysis was sharp, neutral, and grounded in positioning and structure - and I truly appreciated the insights I could apply to my own trading. Posts like the one yesterday on volatility and momentum are genuinely educational, and that’s the kind of value I’m here for.
Since the beginning of April, however, we’ve increasingly leaned into speculative geo-narratives and potential insider information - even while multiple indicators were flashing extreme conditions. I fully agree that the broader macro and geopolitical picture matters and should be considered. But earlier this year, the sub felt more balanced, with a focus on market internals, sentiment, and data. That balance now feels lost or I don't see it. The current tone is much more doomsday-driven, and at times it feels like we’re just waiting for the next bad headline to confirm an already bearish bias. Will we now remain that tone in this sub for the next 3 years until the "real bottom" is called?

Maybe I’ll catch some backlash for sharing this view, and maybe I just “misinterpreted” things. But I still think it’s worth pointing out that failed calls are rarely acknowledged - while the wins are amplified, particularly by the louder voices in the community who always seem to have “read it right” in hindsight.

comment.body:
thank you, i reflected on that.
I explained it that ppl need to distinguish between macro and micro driven structures.
I prepared the macro in early March, the "holy alliance 2.0" plan, as the base case. Also showed in some of the april post, that it was already there in 2019 during Trump's first admin. Also said to follow Kirill Dmitriev and you'll see that I'm not talking out of nowhere...

when volatility is this high as it was, the structural effects are not working. 
Remember the dec fall? vol spiked. No gamma and vanna lvls held that market, but emergency mechanisms. And that was only a small deleveraging, repricing of mispriced expectations.
This April fall is a ways more serious thing: it is a macro-driven, investor-driven dynamics, and a looming bearmarket on stagflation, and global economical repositioning.
This is not easy to follow yeah...

The April posts were almost all about macro in order to give you a POV to digest the informations you will see during the day. Many ppl could do that. But most ppl jsut scrolled through the post. I could see, bcs when for example some ppl asked about the SPX downside target on April 7-8, they didn't recognize that it was literally posted in the weekly post. They clearly didn't read.
Sometimes ppl are asking Qs that are literally written in the post - a clear sign that they don't read. It is okay to not read a long post, but then don't blame me at least, but say "ok, i didn't know bcs i didn't read".

The risk was and is very high.
Now, based on my posts, can you tell, what the current events are telling us?

Earnings were expected to be weak, yeah. Not only by me. It was all described in a Friday post. Were they weak? not really... simply, unwind the position. The bias (the part of the distribution) was derived from that. Like my bet on the soft data print. Did it come soft? - No, I unwound the position and moved on.

Risk/reward on the short side again... why... it was explained many times: bcs short side is underpriced: if one lose on it, he loses small, if he wins, he wins big.
I showed it how i do this, and you can't deny the signaled trades... while i kept signaling SHORT VOL since on april 7; april 13 and later on...
again, this is not my fault if some ppl don't read it.
The purpose of writing a whole post is to not explaining for everybody one by one individually.

I didn't called sub4000 to come... i called sub 4000 to come "IF"...
what i called was consolidation in that range.

and yes, bear market is still there. it is there. and if you understand the macro, you will see... and when market starts to fall again , i will be cursed for some reason.

"small alloc", "big risk"... - yes, bcs one needs to be cautious.
in high vol times it is better to manage the risk. it is up to you to size your position. you get the landscape, you get the educational post about VaR, Sortino etc. size accordingly. 
I'm not somebody who tells you to "go mortgage your house and sell your kids to buy stocks here"

Anyways, facts are facts. Signals and posts are there.
Again: was it perfect? Not at all. Was it real? Yes, absolutely. Was it green? Yes.
And many ppl could follow it. 

I understand that it was hard, but as me along many ppl keep telling: don't trade it if you don't understand it. wait until you get a clearer pic.
If you traded bear markets you know what i'm talking about. 
ppl are eaten up by FOMO now seeing whats already happened.

comment.body:
I get both sides. The market is clearly heading into a bear phase, no doubt about it, and people will realize that later, but they won’t admit that Alma called it. Still, transitions like this are really tricky until the dynamics settle in. A bull market’s easy: just buy every dip. A bear market isn’t. And a lot of folks who aren’t used to it get lost in all the noise, which is frustrating, watching the market stubbornly grind higher while you’re stuck wondering what to do, because it feels overstretched and risky to ride a bull run with no pullbacks. And then people kick themselves thinking  'i should’ve just bought back then” but if you let that go to your head, you’ll blow up your own account.
So yeah, I totally get that it’s tough for a lot of members, because many are inexperienced and uncertain and want someone to hold your hand. But the fact is, Alma never promised to do that, exactly because it’s a lose-lose. Someone’s always going to lose and blame her. Alma navigated this month accurately and laid out the risks clearly. Market raised a ton of big questions. You just have to understand that these processes don’t happen overnight. What kicked off in april is a serious systemic risk. For funds, it’s still a trader’s market. And Alma is right saying wait for at least one narrative to develop before you take bigger risks, don’t just mindlessly long the bottom. It looks easy in hindsight, but back then, it wasn’t. It’s very similar to the 2001 dot-com bubble, with those 10–20% rallies happening while market was still clawing back five years’ worth of returns

comment.body: I think it’s also important to note that if someone gets what Alma’s saying, they shouldn’t keep it to themselves. Too many of those who do understand just attack those who don’t, without even trying to explain anything. Alma never said you can look down on less experienced folks.. she said anyone who unleashes their own frustration on others and attacks without thinking can be told to take a hike. But she also made it clear on the main page that those who know should help those who are less experienced, and that anyone who doesn’t understand should ask questions instead of twisting things and confusing people. What I’ve seen is that most of the chaos here didn’t come from Alma’s posts, but from inexperienced retail investors acting like they know it all. It’s perfectly fine to ask questions, but not to misinterpret and come at someone. I’m trying to help, but it’d be great not to be the only one, and I don’t always have the time

comment.body: I'm always grateful to Alma and mean no criticism. However I do think it's important to learn that when did the current rally change from "artificial one with no real institutional buyers" to some "real?" momentum and buying powers, and how can we read or identify such changes?

comment.body:
I don’t think Alma’s goal is to humiliate anyone, rather she wants to help. She’s admitted that she too needs to improve, because much of what’s obvious to her, and to more seasoned traders, might not be clear to the less experienced. But that doesn’t excuse the inexperienced from using their brains and making an effort to really understand.
For example, Alma regularly shares links in her posts. I wonder how many people have actually gone back and read those references. And her trade signals were indeed accurate..anyone who followed them did better than most over the past month

comment.body:
She does all of this on her own. I think more seasoned traders should step up and help the less experienced, and dial back the condescending remarks. 
Conversely, the newbies need to curb the constant excuse-making and drama, and instead put that energy into self-education. 
Alma doesn’t spoon-feed anybody. Helping isn’t the same as holding your hand

comment.body:
I don’t agree with Sharcus. Everything was laid out in the stack emails. The fact that investors pulled out is a well-confirmed reality, just look at the bond market. Investors don’t think like traders.. Alma wrote a very long post on exactly that.
Then she actually called for vol selling three times: at the current bottom, pre-OPEX, and post-OPEX..she even pinpointed the vanna rally. And she linked right in her post what that means flow-wise, since she’d already explained it in past articles and there are the educational posts to back it up. I think Alma’s baseline assumption is that everyone has read those posts and understood them. But what can she do? She’s got about 700 paid subscribers. That also answers Sharcus’s next point about a flow-driven rebound.
Whether this rally is manufactured or not matters a lot right now. It’s easy to sound smart in hindsight, but back then you had to see it coming, and Alma called vol selling exactly when people were just turning bearish. Don’t forget that. That’s precisely the issue I flagged above

comment.body: When we get Charlie McElligott’s emails there’s a ton of stuff even the pros don’t grasp at first. Charlie lays out a market dynamic from which you can map out possible scenarios for your own portfolio. ut if a PM doesn’t get it and ends up taking a loss, they don’t fire off a note to Charlie calling him a crappy analyst and telling him to go screw himself. That’s the rub. Newbies expect Alma to be some furu who holds their hand through every step.. but she’s not

comment.body: These super long term complex geo+macro theories/comments/conspirations are very interesting but not very useful for trading, no matter the timeframe you trade and when the bias is  so negative and price action does not reflect it at all, it is understandable people are not happy. You can always find an explanation for every move after it happens, every week there is a new pivotal event or narrative. Always will be and always was. The content provided is very complex and information heavy, if there is no clear method on how to utilize this content it is natural to question the usefulness of it, it's most often not a simple if X then Y. Some people might be happy to participate in this geo+macro theorycrafting but if you are daytrader looking to improve your win rate, get better RR or become profitable, you most likely had a hard time in past weeks. These narratives get under your skin especially when explained in such complex and logical way. It is easy to say 'filter it out' but then what is the point? I'm not a billionare middle east investor, I'm a retail trader looking to get accurate under the hood information that I can not figure out myself or read from simple charts. I am looking improve my win rate and entries and I think most people fit that bracket.

comment.body:
I really value all the work Alma puts into her analyses. In practice, though, I mainly rely on the intraday levels as decision points for my trades.
The rest of the content is fascinating andI do my best to absorb the terminology and strategies she discusses, but even after more than a decade of trading, I still feel out of my depth. I’ve never traded options and don’t intend to. I’m just trying to extract what’s useful for my relatively straightforward approach (CFDs - please don’t judge!). It’s surprisingly challenging.
Just my two cents.

comment.body:
Hello everyone, long-time reader, first-time poster here. I can relate to Dmnk’s points and sometimes also struggle to fully follow Alma’s analysis.

Alma, your quick thinking and ability to juggle opposite ideas are impressive, but it can be challenging for some of us to keep up. It’s definitely not a lack of effort or appreciation on our side; we just sometimes need a bit more context or follow-up to connect the dots (as you mentioned also before).

One small suggestion: revisiting previous analyses or clearly updating hypotheses, e.g., "Hypothesis XYZ no longer applies because…" or "Analysis XYZ needs adjustment because…". Concrete examples would clarify how to detect specific conditions or changes. Without this clarity, we’re often left with the general sense ("the market goes down") but unsure about the specifics ("Is it cancelled now?", "Is this how IV/RV should behave?"). This is the most challenging part for me.

Thanks to your insights, I've learned a ton these past months. Hope you’ll consider this feedback. It’d really help bridge the gap.

comment.body: Since 2019 and Trump 1.0 were referenced, it's worth noting that Trump's inflammatory rhetoric and self-worshipping conduct combined to provoke COVID. So, his careless behavior disguised as "art of the deal" makes analysis and forecasting difficult at best (although the coveted "bell" did ring loudly at the February 2020 top. Bottom line: the difficulty with market forecasting has increased since April as Trump has Increased the frequency of his unbalanced rhetoric and verbal market manipulation. Alma has done the best she could under those conditions
